one_line_takeaway: Isolating raw tool outputs from an LLM agent's trusted memory via hierarchical worker agents and schema-validated JSON returns — OS-style process isolation applied to agent context — cuts indirect-prompt-injection ASR from 30.66% to 0.78% on AgentDojo while slightly improving benign utility, and ablations show memory isolation alone (no validator/sanitizer) already achieves 2.19% ASR.
key_finding: On AgentDojo (GPT-4o-mini), AGENTSYS achieves 0.78% ASR, 64.36% benign utility, and 52.87% attacked utility, versus 30.66% ASR / 63.54% benign utility / 48.27% attacked utility for No Defense — i.e., it reduces ASR by ~97% while slightly improving benign utility. On ASB it achieves 4.25% ASR. Ablation shows hierarchical context isolation alone (no validator, no sanitizer) already achieves 2.19% ASR and 56.10% benign utility, versus 30.66%/63.54% undefended — the paper's central claim that architectural memory isolation is the dominant security mechanism, independent of detection or sanitization. Removing context isolation while keeping validator+sanitizer (i.e., reverting to a standard ReAct agent with sanitized appends) raises ASR to 8.62% and drops benign utility to 62.49%, roughly 10x worse ASR than full AGENTSYS. Under adaptive attacks targeting the schema-bounded channel specifically, ASR rises modestly from 0.78% (Base) to 1.43% (manual adaptive) and 2.06% (PAIR-refined), still >93% lower than the undefended baseline; per-scenario PAIR ASR peaks at 6.94% in Banking. AGENTSYS attains 0% ASR on tasks requiring ≥4 tool calls (Fig. 4b) and shows only a 38.21% utility drop from short to long tasks, versus 60.96% for DRIFT and 52.98% for Progent, i.e., markedly more graceful degradation on long-horizon tasks. Token overhead is 3.25M on AgentDojo (vs. 0.82M undefended, 6.09M for CaMeL), yielding the highest 'Defense Quality' score (63.86) among all evaluated methods.
core_contribution: AGENTSYS, a system-level defense against indirect prompt injection (IPI) in LLM agents that enforces explicit, hierarchical memory management. The main (trusted) agent never appends raw tool outputs to its context; instead, each tool call spawns a short-lived worker agent that operates in an isolated context (no user query, no long-horizon memory), guided only by a pre-declared JSON-schema 'intent' the main agent commits to before seeing the tool output. Only schema-validated, JSON-parsable return values cross back into the main agent's memory. Workers may recursively spawn nested workers to complete multi-step extraction, but any further tool call they attempt is gated by an LLM-based validator that sees only the original user query and a compact call-trace (never raw tool outputs), applied selectively via event-triggered checks on 'command' (side-effecting) tools rather than all tools (a CQRS-inspired read/write taxonomy computed once per toolset). On validator denial, a sanitizer strips instruction-like spans from the untrusted output and the worker retries extraction under a bounded per-subtask budget before failing explicitly. The design is explicitly framed as inspired by OS-style process memory isolation.
threat_model: Indirect prompt injection: an adversary who can only influence tool-returned observations y (e.g., poisoned web pages, emails, files, API responses) but cannot modify the tool executor or environment transition function. The adversary's goal is to steer the agent's subsequent policy decisions by embedding instructions that, once admitted into the agent's working memory, persist across reasoning rounds. The paper formalizes an attack as occurring when a contaminated trace diverges from the clean trace (either in actions taken or observations produced). The adaptive-attacker variant assumes the attacker additionally knows AGENTSYS's architecture and specifically targets the schema-bounded worker-to-main communication channel (i.e., tries to smuggle malicious key-value pairs into the structured JSON the worker returns).
limitations: The paper's own ablations show the validator alone (sanitize-everything, no gating) achieves the lowest standalone ASR (0.18%) but at large utility cost (50.85% benign utility), and removing the sanitizer entirely raises ASR to 1.54% with utility dropping to 57.66% — the components trade off against each other and full AGENTSYS is a tuned combination rather than a strictly dominant design in every ablation cell. The schema-validated return channel is explicitly acknowledged as still leaky: string-valued fields in the return object 'may still contain attacker-controlled content,' so the contract narrows rather than eliminates the channel. Adaptive-attack evaluation, while present, includes only one manually crafted attack template and one automated method (PAIR); ASR under adaptive attack rises to nearly 7% in the Banking scenario specifically, showing scenario-dependent residual risk. The paper does not report results on ASB across all six foundation models (only AgentDojo gets the full 6-model sweep, per the text referencing Tables 5-7); ASB headline numbers appear to be single-model (GPT-4o-mini). The validator/sanitizer/worker mechanisms each add LLM calls, so overhead (3.25M tokens vs 0.82M undefended, ~4x) is nontrivial even if favorable relative to some system-level baselines like CaMeL (6.09M tokens).

datasets_used: AgentDojo (4 scenarios: Banking, Slack, Travel, Workspace; 97 user tasks, 629 injection tasks)
quantitative_results: AgentDojo, GPT-4o-mini: AGENTSYS ASR 0.78%, Benign Utility 64.36%, Attacked Utility 52.87%
AgentDojo, GPT-4o-mini: No Defense ASR 30.66%, Benign Utility 63.54%, Attacked Utility 48.27%
Ablation w/o Context Isolation: ASR 8.62%, Benign Utility 62.49%
Ablation w/o Validator: ASR 0.18%, Benign Utility 50.85%
Ablation w/o Sanitizer: ASR 1.54%, Benign Utility 57.66%
Ablation w/o Validator and Sanitizer (context isolation only): ASR 2.19%, Benign Utility 56.10%
Adaptive attacks, Banking scenario: ASR up to 6.94% under PAIR
AGENTSYS achieves 0% ASR on tasks with trajectory length >= 4 tool calls

Benign utility by trajectory length: AGENTSYS 39.78% (short, <=3 calls) vs 24.58% (long, >3 calls), a 38.21% relative drop, vs. No Defense 44.46%->19.08% (57% drop), DRIFT 35.52%->16.70% (60.96% drop), Progent 37.65%->14.70% (52.98% drop)

Token overhead on AgentDojo: AGENTSYS 3.25M tokens (Defense Quality 63.86); No Defense 0.82M tokens (Defense Quality 44.06); CaMeL 6.09M tokens (Defense Quality 29.97); Progent 2.60M tokens (Defense Quality 58.87); DRIFT 2.37M tokens (Defense Quality 57.73)
Baseline persistence motivation: for trajectory length 4, injection at round 1 achieves 60.53% ASR vs 15.38% at round 2, 5.88% at round 3, 0% at round 4 (undefended agent)
